Professor View 01 / 40
Business English -- 60 Aulas

Maria Claudia Curimbaba

De executiva que traduz mentalmente para o inglês a líder que conduz reuniões internacionais com autoridade e precisão

B1+ / B2 inicial São Paulo, SP Gestora / Sócia de Holding Familiar 60 min / 2x semana / Presencial
Progresso Geral0%
Speed:
Maria Claudia Curimbaba
47 anos
Gestora / Sócia de Holding Familiar
São Paulo, SP
B1+ / B2 inicial
Business English — Reuniões e Negociação
60 aulas de 60 min
Presencial — 2x/semana

Jornada de Transformação

De: Executiva que traduz mentalmente do português para o inglês, perde o timing em reuniões internacionais e depende de colegas para intermediar conversas com parceiros em Houston e Pittsburgh

Para: Líder que conduz reuniões com sócios e parceiros internacionais, negocia contratos, apresenta resultados financeiros e toma decisões estratégicas em inglês com autoridade e precisão

Promessa do Programa

Em 60 aulas, Maria Claudia vai deixar de traduzir mentalmente e passar a pensar, reagir e decidir em inglês. Vai conduzir reuniões com Houston e Pittsburgh, negociar contratos de joint venture e apresentar resultados no board com a mesma autoridade que já tem em português.

Forças

  • Forte experiência em gestão e negociação
  • Conhecimento profundo do vocabulário de negócios em português
  • Motivação alta: precisa do inglês para o dia a dia
  • Exposição regular a parceiros anglófonos
  • Disciplinada e comprometida com o processo

Pontos de Desenvolvimento

  • Tradução mental constante (português para inglês)
  • Vocabulário corporativo limitado em inglês
  • Insegurança ao falar em reuniões formais
  • Dificuldade com voz passiva e estruturas complexas
  • Pronúncia de termos técnicos financeiros

19Houston We Have Culture — US vs Brazil in Business and ArtCultural comparisonsCultural exchange RP (museums + meetings)Research Houston Museum District
20Review and Integration — Block 2All structures reviewedFull negotiation scenarioSelf-assessment recording

27Logistics and Shipping — Industrial VocabularyPassive with modalsLogistics problem-solvingLabel logistics flowchart

29Q and A Mastery — Fielding Difficult QuestionsHedging languageQ and A hot seat simulationWrite anticipated Q and A
30Review and Integration — Block 3All structures reviewedFull presentation and Q and ASelf-assessment recording

36Conditional Mastery — If We Shipped by FridayMixed conditionalsConditional negotiationWrite conditional proposals

40Review and Integration — Block 4All structures reviewedFull simulation: Houston dinner + gallerySelf-assessment recording

Welcome to Your English Journey

Maria Claudia, this is your Pre-class space. Complete these exercises before each lesson to maximize your learning. If you ever get stuck during a conversation, use these emergency phrases:

1 Could you repeat that, please?
2 I am not sure I understand. Could you explain?
3 Let me think about that for a moment.
4 How do you say this in English?
5 Could you speak more slowly, please?
Aula 01 — Pre-class

Who Is Maria Claudia? — Diagnostic + Executive Self-Portrait

Build your executive vocabulary and practice introducing yourself in English
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary
Oversee.to manage and supervise from a high position
"I oversee the financial and sales operations of the holding."
Holding (Company).a parent company that owns other companies
"Our holding company owns three subsidiaries in different sectors."
Subsidiary.a company owned or controlled by a parent company
"The subsidiary in Houston reports directly to us."
Counterpart.a person in the same position at another company
"My counterpart in Pittsburgh handles manufacturing operations."
Stakeholder.a person with an interest in the business
"All stakeholders must approve the annual budget."
Joint Venture.a business arrangement between two companies sharing ownership
"We are planning a joint venture with a Texas-based company."
Procurement.the process of buying goods or services for a business
"The procurement process takes about three months."
Revenue.the total income a company generates
"Our revenue increased by twelve percent last quarter."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its correct definition.

Oversee
Holding (Company)
Subsidiary
Counterpart
Stakeholder
Joint Venture
Procurement
Revenue

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Maria Claudia oversees the financial operations of a family holding company in Sao Paulo. She is responsible for three subsidiaries that operate in different sectors. Every month, she deals with her counterparts in Houston and Pittsburgh to review revenue targets and procurement contracts. The stakeholders expect quarterly reports, and Maria Claudia is currently working on a joint venture with a new partner in Texas.

1. What does Maria Claudia oversee?
A The marketing department
B The financial operations of the holding
C Only the Houston subsidiary
2. How often does she deal with her counterparts?
A Every week
B Every day
C Every month
3. What is Maria Claudia currently working on?
A A new marketing campaign
B Closing the Pittsburgh office
C A joint venture with a Texas partner

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
Present Simple vs Present Continuous
Present Simple Habitual actions, routines, facts
I oversee the operations. Always, as a routine — habitual action
She deals with partners. Regular, repeated activity
Present Continuous Current projects, temporary actions
I am working on a joint venture. Right now, temporary project
We are planning an expansion. Current, ongoing project

Tip: Use the Present Simple for what you do regularly (routine) and the Present Continuous for what is happening right now or as a temporary project. Key words: always, usually, every day = Present Simple. Currently, right now, at the moment = Present Continuous.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice
"The holding company all subsidiaries."
"She with stakeholders on a weekly basis."
"The department reports to Maria Claudia."
" from the Houston subsidiary grew last year."
"Her in Pittsburgh manages the factory."
"They are planning a new this year."
"The needs to increase its revenue."
"All approved the new budget."

Stage 2: Vocabulary Practice — Meeting Introduction

Order

Put these phrases in the correct order to form a professional meeting introduction.

? "I deal with international partners in Houston and Pittsburgh."
? "Good morning. Let me introduce myself."
? "I am currently working on expanding our joint ventures."
? "I am responsible for managing three subsidiaries."
? "Our revenue grew twelve percent last quarter."
? "I am Maria Claudia. I oversee the financial and sales operations of our holding."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking
I oversee the operations of our holding company.
I am responsible for three subsidiaries in different sectors.
I deal with counterparts in Houston and Pittsburgh every month.
Our revenue grew twelve percent last quarter.
I am currently working on a joint venture with a Texas partner.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You meet a new partner at a business dinner. How do you introduce your role?
A "I work with some companies."
B "I oversee the financial and sales operations of our holding company."
C "I am the boss of everything."
D "I do business things in Brazil."
2. Your Houston counterpart asks about your current projects. What do you say?
A "I work on something."
B "I am doing many things now."
C "I am currently working on expanding our joint ventures in the region."
D "I currently work on expand the joint ventures."
3. A stakeholder asks about revenue. Which is the most professional answer?
A "Money is good this year."
B "Our revenue increased by twelve percent last quarter."
C "We make more money than before."
D "The revenue is being good."
4. Someone asks where you work. What is the best response?
A "I am based in Sao Paulo, and I manage operations across three subsidiaries."
B "I am in Sao Paulo."
C "My city is Sao Paulo."
D "I live and work in the Brazil."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are at a meeting with a new partner from Houston. Introduce yourself and your company in 2 minutes. Include: your name, your role, what you oversee, your subsidiaries, and what you are currently working on.

Survival Card — Lesson 1

1 Let me introduce myself. I am Maria Claudia from Sao Paulo.
2 I oversee the financial and sales operations of our holding.
3 I am responsible for managing three subsidiaries.
4 I deal with international partners in Houston and Pittsburgh.
5 I am currently working on expanding our joint ventures.
Aula 02 — Pre-class

The Holding Structure — Describing Your Business Empire

Learn to describe your company structure with precision
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary
Headquarters.the main office where a company is managed from
"Our headquarters is located in downtown Sao Paulo."
Branch.a local office or division of a larger company
"We have two branches in the United States."
Board (of Directors).the group of people who govern a company
"The board of directors approved the expansion plan."
Shareholder.a person who owns part of a company
"Each shareholder receives a quarterly dividend report."
Merge.to combine two companies into one
"The two companies decided to merge last year."
Acquire.to buy or take over another company
"We plan to acquire a logistics company in Texas."
Division.a major section or department of a company
"The technology division is our fastest-growing unit."
Assets.valuable properties or resources owned by a company
"The company's assets include real estate and patents."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its correct English definition.

Headquarters
Branch
Board
Shareholder
Merge
Acquire
Division
Assets

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar
Maria Claudia is presenting her holding company to a potential partner from Houston. She explains that the company's headquarters is located in Sao Paulo. The board of directors meets every quarter to review the company's assets and approve new investments. There are three main divisions under the holding. The technology division is the largest, and there is a new logistics branch in Texas. The company's shareholders voted to acquire a small firm in Pittsburgh last month. There are also plans to merge two of the smaller subsidiaries into one stronger division.
1. Where is the company's headquarters located?
A Houston
B Sao Paulo
C Pittsburgh
D Texas
2. How many main divisions are there under the holding?
A Two
B Four
C Three
D Five
3. What did the shareholders vote to do last month?
A Merge the headquarters
B Close a branch in Texas
C Acquire a small firm in Pittsburgh
D Sell the company's assets

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
Possessives: 's and s'
Rule Example
Singular noun + 's The company's headquarters = the headquarters of the company
Plural noun ending in s + ' The shareholders' meeting = the meeting of the shareholders
Irregular plural + 's The board's decision = the decision of the board
There is / There are
Structure Usage Example
There is Singular nouns There is a new branch in Texas.
There are Plural nouns There are three divisions under the holding.

Key Expressions

Vocabulary
Our headquarters is located in...
"Our headquarters is located in Sao Paulo, and we manage operations across three countries."
The board is responsible for...
"The board is responsible for approving major investments and strategic decisions."
We have branches in...
"We have branches in Houston, Pittsburgh, and soon in Miami."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Use the hints if you need help.

"The holding's is in Sao Paulo."
"There three divisions under the main company."
"The decision was to acquire a new firm."
"There a new branch in Texas."
"The meeting is scheduled for June."
"The company's include patents and real estate."
"There two branches in the United States."
"The revenue grew by fifteen percent."

Stage 2: Vocabulary Practice

Order

Put these sentences in the correct order to form a company description presentation.

? "The board of directors meets quarterly to review our performance."
? "Good morning. Let me describe our company structure."
? "We are currently looking to acquire a new logistics firm."
? "There are three main divisions: technology, logistics, and finance."
? "Our headquarters is located in Sao Paulo, Brazil."
? "We have branches in Houston and Pittsburgh."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. Aim for 80% accuracy or higher.

Our headquarters is located in downtown Sao Paulo, Brazil.
The board of directors is responsible for approving all major investments.
There are three divisions under our holding company.
We have branches in Houston, Pittsburgh, and Texas.
The shareholders approved the plan to acquire a new firm.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You are at a conference and an investor asks about your company location. What do you say?
A "We have assets in many places."
B "Our headquarters is located in Sao Paulo."
C "The board decided to move last year."
D "We merged with a Sao Paulo company."
2. A partner asks who makes the big decisions at your company. What do you answer?
A "The branch in Houston handles everything."
B "Our assets decide the strategy."
C "The board of directors is responsible for all major decisions."
D "We acquire companies to make decisions."
3. You want to explain that your company has offices in several cities. What is the best way to say it?
A "There is branches in many cities."
B "We merged branches in Houston."
C "The division has one office."
D "We have branches in Houston, Pittsburgh, and Miami."
4. Your company recently bought another company. How do you describe this to a colleague?
A "We merged our headquarters with Texas."
B "The shareholders are new assets."
C "We acquired a logistics company in Texas last quarter."
D "The board divided the branch."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are at a conference. A potential investor asks: "Can you describe your company structure?" Record yourself describing your holding in 2 minutes. Try to use: headquarters, branch, board, shareholders, divisions, and assets.

Survival Card — Lesson 2

1 Our headquarters is located in Sao Paulo.
2 The holding has three subsidiaries.
3 The board meets quarterly to review our assets.
4 We are looking to acquire a new company.
5 There are branches in Houston and Pittsburgh.
Aula 03 — Pre-class

Running the Meeting

Master meeting vocabulary: agenda, minutes, action items, deadlines. Learn to open, facilitate, and close meetings with confidence.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary

Vocabulary
Agenda • a list of topics to discuss in a meeting
"We need to review the agenda before the meeting starts."
Minutes • the official written record of a meeting
"Please send the minutes to all participants by end of day."
Action item • a specific task assigned to someone during a meeting
"Each action item must have a clear owner and deadline."
Deadline • the final date by which something must be completed
"The deadline for the procurement report is next Friday."
Adjourn • to formally end a meeting
"If there are no more questions, let's adjourn."
Facilitate • to guide or manage a discussion or meeting
"Maria Claudia will facilitate the quarterly review today."
Follow up • to check on progress or take further action after a meeting
"I will follow up on the pending items next Monday."
Wrap up • to bring a discussion or meeting to a close
"Let's wrap up this discussion and move to the next topic."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice
Agenda
Minutes
Action item
Deadline
Adjourn
Facilitate
Follow up
Wrap up

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar
Maria Claudia is facilitating a quarterly review meeting with her team and counterparts from Houston. She opens the meeting by reviewing the agenda. "Let's begin with the revenue update. Please look at the numbers on your screen. Let's move on to the procurement report. Take note of the new deadlines. Each action item must have an owner. Please send the minutes by end of day. Let's adjourn and follow up next week."
1. What is Maria Claudia doing in this meeting?
A Presenting her holding company
B Facilitating a quarterly review
C Negotiating a contract
D Interviewing a new employee
2. What does Maria Claudia ask the team to do with the minutes?
A Read them before the meeting
B Delete them after the meeting
C Send them by end of day
D Print them for the next meeting
3. What must each action item have?
A A budget
B A presentation
C An owner
D A separate meeting

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
Imperatives: Giving Instructions
Structure Usage Example
Base verb (direct) Clear command Take note of the action items.
Please + verb (polite) Professional request Please send the minutes by Friday.
Do not + verb (negative) Prohibition Do not forget the deadline.
Let's + Verb: Group Suggestions
Structure Usage Example
Let's + verb Positive suggestion Let's review the agenda.
Let's not + verb Negative suggestion Let's not skip any items.

Key Expressions

Vocabulary
Let's move on to...
"Let's move on to the procurement report and review the new deadlines."
Let's wrap up...
"Let's wrap up the budget discussion and assign action items to each team."
Please make sure to...
"Please make sure to send the updated numbers to Houston before Thursday."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Use the hints if you need help.

"Let's begin with the first on the agenda."
"Please send the by end of day."
"Let's this discussion and move to the next topic."
" note of the action items."
"The for the procurement report is next Friday."
"Maria Claudia will the meeting today."
"Let's on the pending items next Monday."
"If there are no more questions, let's ."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put these meeting steps in the correct order.

? "Let's begin with the revenue update from Q3."
? "Let's assign action items before we finish."
? "Good morning, everyone. Let's get started."
? "Please send the minutes by end of day. Meeting adjourned."
? "First, let's review the agenda for today."
? "Please take note of the new procurement deadlines."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen, then record yourself saying each phrase. Compare your pronunciation.

Let's begin with the first item on the agenda.
Please send the minutes to all participants by end of day.
Let's move on to the next topic on the agenda.
Take note of the action items and their deadlines.
Let's adjourn. I will follow up on Friday.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You are opening a meeting. What do you say?
A "I am responsible for the financial operations."
B "Let's begin with the agenda for today."
C "There are three divisions under the holding."
D "Our headquarters is located in Sao Paulo."
2. You want the team to discuss the next topic. What do you say?
A "The deadline is next Friday."
B "Please adjourn the meeting."
C "Let's move on to the procurement report."
D "I deal with international partners."
3. You need someone to send the meeting notes. What do you say?
A "Let's acquire a new firm."
B "Take note of the shareholders."
C "There is a new branch in Texas."
D "Please send the minutes by Friday."
4. You want to end the meeting. What do you say?
A "Let's follow up next week."
B "If there are no more questions, let's adjourn."
C "Our revenue increased by twelve percent."
D "I am currently working on a joint venture."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are facilitating a quarterly review meeting with your Houston counterpart. How would you open the meeting, introduce the agenda, and assign action items? Record your response.

Survival Card — Lesson 3

1 Let's begin with the first item on the agenda.
2 Could we move on to the next topic?
3 Please send the minutes by end of day.
4 Let's assign action items before we adjourn.
5 I will follow up on this by Friday.
Aula 04 — Pre-class

Money Talks

Master financial vocabulary: revenue, profit, budget, forecast. Learn to compare numbers and present financial results.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary

Vocabulary
Revenue • the total income a company earns from sales
"Our revenue increased by twelve percent last quarter."
Profit • the money remaining after all expenses are paid
"The company reported a net profit of two million dollars."
Budget • a financial plan that outlines expected income and spending
"We need to approve the budget for next quarter."
Forecast • a prediction of future financial performance
"The forecast shows steady growth through Q4."
Expenses • the costs of running a business
"Our operating expenses are lower than last year."
Growth • an increase in value, revenue, or market size
"We achieved ten percent growth compared to Q2."
Margin • the percentage difference between revenue and costs
"Our profit margin improved from eight to twelve percent."
Quarter • a three-month period in the business year (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4)
"Q3 results exceeded our expectations."

Expressions

Vocabulary
Compared to last quarter...
"Compared to last quarter, our revenue is up twelve percent."
The numbers show that...
"The numbers show that our profit margin has improved significantly."
We are on track to...
"We are on track to exceed our annual revenue target."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice
Revenue
Profit
Budget
Forecast
Expenses
Growth
Margin
Quarter

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar
Maria Claudia is presenting the Q3 financial results to the board. Revenue is higher than Q2 by twelve percent. The profit margin improved from eight percent to twelve percent. Operating expenses are lower than last year because the procurement team negotiated better contracts. The Houston subsidiary showed the strongest growth, with revenue up fifteen percent compared to Q2. The forecast for Q4 is positive, and the budget has been approved. Overall, Q3 results are better than expected.
1. How does Q3 revenue compare to Q2?
A It is lower than Q2
B It is higher than Q2 by twelve percent
C It is the same as Q2
D It decreased compared to Q2
2. Why are operating expenses lower than last year?
A The company reduced its workforce
B They moved to a smaller office
C The procurement team negotiated better contracts
D The Houston subsidiary closed
3. Which subsidiary showed the strongest growth?
A The Pittsburgh subsidiary
B The Houston subsidiary
C The Texas subsidiary
D The Sao Paulo headquarters

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
Comparatives: Comparing Numbers and Results
Structure Usage Example
higher than Compare larger values Revenue is higher than last quarter.
lower than Compare smaller values Expenses are lower than last year.
better than Compare quality/results Q3 results are better than expected.
worse than Compare negative results Q1 performance was worse than Q4.
more/less + noun + than Compare quantities We have more revenue than last year.
as + adj + as Compare equal values Q3 was as strong as we expected.
Numbers in Financial English
Term Example
percent twelve percent growth
million two million dollars
billion one billion in revenue
point (decimal) eight point five percent

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice
"Our revenue is than last quarter."
"Operating decreased by five percent."
"The profit improved from eight to twelve percent."
"The for Q4 shows steady growth."
"We need to approve the for next year."
"Q3 exceeded two million dollars."
"The Houston subsidiary achieved fifteen percent ."
"Q3 results are than expected."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order
? "Our operating expenses were lower than expected."
? "First, our total revenue for Q3 was twelve million dollars."
? "The forecast for Q4 shows continued growth."
? "Good morning. Let's review the Q3 financial results."
? "The profit margin improved from eight to twelve percent."
? "Compared to Q2, that is an increase of twelve percent."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking
Our revenue increased by twelve percent compared to last quarter.
The profit margin is higher than Q2, at twelve percent.
Operating expenses are lower than last year.
We are on track to exceed our annual targets.
The forecast for Q4 shows steady growth.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. Your revenue went from 10M to 12M. How do you describe this?
A "Our revenue decreased this quarter."
B "Our revenue is higher than last quarter."
C "Our revenue stayed the same."
2. Expenses went from 5M to 4M. What happened?
A "Our expenses are higher than before."
B "Our expenses are lower than last year."
C "Our expenses remained unchanged."
3. You want to show Q4 predictions. What do you present?
A "The budget for Q4."
B "The forecast for Q4."
C "The expenses for Q4."
4. The board asks about profitability. What do you report?
A "Our revenue increased by twelve percent."
B "Our profit margin improved to twelve percent."
C "Our budget was approved for next quarter."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are presenting the Q3 financial results to the board. Describe the revenue, expenses, profit margin, and forecast. Use comparatives to show how Q3 compares to Q2.

Survival Card — Lesson 4

1 Our revenue increased by twelve percent this quarter.
2 Compared to last year, our profit margin is higher.
3 The budget for next quarter is three million dollars.
4 We are on track to meet our annual targets.
5 The forecast shows steady growth for Q4.
Aula 05 — Pre-class

Colors and Opinions

Describing Art, Expressing Preferences. Learn adjective order (OSASCOMP) and how to express opinions using "I find it + adjective".
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary

Vocabulary
Striking • very attractive or noticeable
"The painting has a striking use of color."
Abstract • art that does not represent real objects
"She prefers abstract art over realistic paintings."
Exhibit • a public display of art or objects
"The museum has a new exhibit on modern art."
Contemporary • belonging to the present time
"Contemporary art often challenges traditional ideas."
Vibrant • bright and strong, full of energy
"The artist uses vibrant colors like red and orange."
Subtle • not obvious, delicate and understated
"The subtle tones in the background create depth."
Composition • the arrangement of elements in a work of art
"The composition draws your eye to the center."
Palette • the range of colors used by an artist
"The artist chose a warm palette of earth tones."
Expressions
I find it...
"I find it fascinating how the artist uses light and shadow."
What strikes me is...
"What strikes me is the contrast between the warm and cool colors."
In my opinion...
"In my opinion, this is one of the most powerful pieces in the exhibit."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice
Striking
Abstract
Exhibit
Contemporary
Vibrant
Subtle
Composition
Palette

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar
Maria Claudia is visiting a contemporary art exhibit in Houston with her counterpart David Chen. She stops in front of a large abstract painting with vibrant colors. "I find it fascinating," she says. "What strikes me is the bold composition. The artist uses a warm palette of reds and oranges. In my opinion, this is the most striking piece in the exhibit." David points to a smaller, more subtle painting nearby. "I find it elegant," he says. "The composition is simpler, but the colors are more refined. It is a beautiful old European oil painting."
1. What does Maria Claudia think about the large abstract painting?
A She finds it boring
B She finds it fascinating
C She finds it too simple
D She does not like the colors
2. What palette does the artist of the large painting use?
A A cool palette of blues and greens
B A warm palette of reds and oranges
C A neutral palette of grays and whites
D A dark palette of blacks and browns
3. How does David describe the smaller painting?
A He finds it striking and bold
B He finds it elegant with refined colors
C He finds it confusing and abstract
D He does not like it at all

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
Adjective Order (OSASCOMP)
Order Category Example
1Opinionbeautiful, striking, elegant
2Sizelarge, small, tiny
3Ageold, new, contemporary
4Shaperound, rectangular, abstract
5Colorred, vibrant, dark
6OriginEuropean, Japanese, Brazilian
7Materialoil, watercolor, acrylic
8Purposedecorative, commemorative
"I find it + adjective"
Structure Example
I find it + adjectiveI find it beautiful.
I find it + adjective + how/thatI find it interesting how the artist uses light.
I find it + adjective (opinion)I find it thought-provoking.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice
"I find it how the artist uses light."
"The painting has a use of color and texture."
"She prefers art over realistic paintings."
"The museum has a new on modern art."
"The artist uses colors like red and orange."
"The tones in the background create depth."
"The draws your eye to the center of the painting."
"The artist chose a warm of earth tones."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order
? "What strikes me is the vibrant palette the artist chose."
? "Let me know which painting you find most interesting."
? "Welcome to the exhibit. Let me show you around."
? "In my opinion, this is the most striking piece in the collection."
? "This is a contemporary abstract painting from 2023."
? "I find it fascinating how the composition guides your eye."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking
I find it fascinating how the artist uses light and shadow.
What strikes me is the contrast between warm and cool colors.
The exhibit features contemporary abstract paintings.
In my opinion, the composition is very powerful.
The artist chose a vibrant palette of reds and oranges.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You see a painting with bold, bright colors. How do you describe it?
A "The painting has subtle colors."
B "The painting has vibrant colors."
C "The painting has no colors."
2. Someone asks your opinion about art. How do you respond?
A "I have no opinion about it."
B "I find it thought-provoking."
C "The revenue increased last quarter."
3. You want to describe the arrangement of elements. What word do you use?
A "The palette is well-balanced."
B "The composition is well-balanced."
C "The exhibit is well-balanced."
4. You visit a museum display. What is it called?
A "An exhibit."
B "A palette."
C "A composition."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are visiting an art exhibit with your Houston counterpart. Describe your favorite painting: the colors, the composition, and why you find it interesting.

Survival Card — Lesson 5

1 I find it fascinating.
2 What strikes me is the use of color.
3 In my opinion, this is a beautiful piece.
4 The artist uses a vibrant palette.
5 The composition is very striking.
Aula 06 — Pre-class

The Passive Voice

Reports Are Written, Paintings Are Exhibited. Learn the present passive (is/are + past participle) to describe processes and procedures.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary

Vocabulary
Submit • to present a document or report for review
"Reports are submitted to the board every quarter."
Approve • to officially accept or agree to something
"The budget is approved by the shareholders."
Review • to examine or assess something carefully
"Financial statements are reviewed by the auditors."
Assign • to give someone a specific task or responsibility
"Action items are assigned at the end of each meeting."
Display • to show or exhibit something publicly
"Contemporary paintings are displayed in the main gallery."
Commission • to formally request someone to create a work of art
"New artworks are commissioned by the museum every year."
Distribute • to share or deliver something to multiple people
"Meeting minutes are distributed to all participants."
Evaluate • to assess the quality or value of something
"Employee performance is evaluated twice a year."
Expressions
It is done by...
"The report is reviewed by the finance team before submission."
It is considered...
"This painting is considered one of the most important works of the century."
It has been...
"The budget has been approved by the board."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice
Submit
Approve
Review
Assign
Display
Commission
Distribute
Evaluate

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar
Maria Claudia is explaining how her company operates to a new partner. Reports are submitted to the board every quarter. The annual budget is approved by the shareholders in January. Financial statements are reviewed by external auditors. Action items are assigned at the end of each meeting, and meeting minutes are distributed to all participants within 24 hours. On the art side, Maria Claudia explains that contemporary paintings are displayed in the company's lobby. New artworks are commissioned every two years. Each piece is evaluated by a committee before it is purchased.
1. How often are reports submitted to the board?
A Every month
B Every quarter
C Every year
D Every week
2. Who approves the annual budget?
A The auditors
B The shareholders
C The committee
D The museum
3. How often are new artworks commissioned?
A Every quarter
B Every year
C Every two years
D Every five years

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
Present Passive (is/are + past participle)
Active Voice Passive Voice
The board approves the budget.The budget is approved by the board.
The museum displays the paintings.The paintings are displayed in the museum.
The auditors review the statements.The statements are reviewed by the auditors.
They distribute the minutes.The minutes are distributed to all participants.
Structure
Component Example
Subject + is/are + past participleThe budget is approved.
+ by agent (optional)The budget is approved by the board.
Present perfect passiveThe budget has been approved by the board.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice
"Reports are to the board every quarter."
"The budget is by the shareholders."
"Financial statements are by the auditors."
"Action items are at the end of each meeting."
"Contemporary paintings are in the main gallery."
"New artworks are by the museum every year."
"Meeting minutes are to all participants."
"Employee performance is twice a year."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order
? "The final version is submitted to the board."
? "The report is written by the finance team."
? "Copies are distributed to all stakeholders."
? "Corrections are made based on the feedback."
? "The report is approved by the shareholders."
? "It is reviewed by the department manager."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking
Reports are submitted to the board every quarter.
The budget is approved by the shareholders in January.
Contemporary paintings are displayed in our lobby.
Meeting minutes are distributed within 24 hours.
Each artwork is evaluated before it is purchased.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. Who approves the budget?
A "The budget is approved by the auditors."
B "The budget is approved by the shareholders."
C "The budget is approved by the committee."
2. How are meeting notes shared?
A "Minutes are submitted to the board."
B "Minutes are distributed to all participants."
C "Minutes are evaluated by the manager."
3. Where are the paintings shown?
A "They are displayed in the main gallery."
B "They are submitted to the museum."
C "They are assigned to the committee."
4. How often is performance checked?
A "It is evaluated every quarter."
B "It is evaluated twice a year."
C "It is evaluated every month."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Describe how a financial report moves through your company. Use the passive voice: it is written, reviewed, submitted, approved, and distributed.

Survival Card — Lesson 6

1 The report is submitted every quarter.
2 The budget has been approved by the board.
3 Paintings are displayed in the main gallery.
4 Minutes are distributed after every meeting.
5 New artworks are commissioned every two years.
Aula 07 — Pre-class

Negotiation 101

Prices, Terms, Conditions. Learn the first conditional (If + present simple, will + base verb) to propose terms, make counteroffers, and reach agreements.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary

Vocabulary
Negotiate • to discuss terms in order to reach an agreement
"We need to negotiate the price before signing the contract."
Proposal • a formal plan or suggestion presented for consideration
"The vendor submitted a proposal for the new logistics contract."
Counteroffer • a response to an offer with different terms
"We will make a counteroffer if the price is too high."
Discount • a reduction in the usual price
"If you order in bulk, we will offer a ten percent discount."
Terms • the conditions of an agreement or contract
"The payment terms are net 30 days."
Clause • a specific section or condition in a contract
"There is a penalty clause for late delivery."
Leverage • an advantage used to influence a negotiation
"Our volume gives us leverage to negotiate better prices."
Concession • something given up in a negotiation to reach agreement
"We made a concession on the delivery timeline."
Expressions
If you agree to..., we will...
"If you agree to a two-year contract, we will offer a fifteen percent discount."
We are willing to...
"We are willing to extend the payment terms to 60 days."
On the condition that...
"We will accept the price on the condition that delivery is guaranteed by March."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice
Negotiate
Proposal
Counteroffer
Discount
Terms
Clause
Leverage
Concession

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar
Maria Claudia is negotiating a new logistics contract with a vendor in Texas. She reviews the proposal carefully. If the price is too high, she will make a counteroffer. The vendor offers a five percent discount, but Maria Claudia wants ten percent. "If you agree to a two-year contract, we will increase the discount to ten percent," the vendor says. Maria Claudia considers the terms. There is a penalty clause for late delivery, which gives her leverage. "We are willing to sign for two years on the condition that delivery is guaranteed within five business days," she replies. If both sides make concessions, they will reach an agreement.
1. What will Maria Claudia do if the price is too high?
A She will accept the price.
B She will make a counteroffer.
C She will cancel the contract.
D She will ask for a new vendor.
2. What does the vendor offer if Maria Claudia agrees to a two-year contract?
A Free delivery
B A ten percent discount
C Extended payment terms
D A five percent discount
3. What gives Maria Claudia leverage in the negotiation?
A The two-year contract
B The discount percentage
C The penalty clause for late delivery
D The vendor's proposal

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
First Conditional (If + present simple, will + base verb)
If clause (present simple) Main clause (will + base verb)
If we increase the order,the vendor will offer a discount.
If they accept our terms,we will sign the contract.
If we do not negotiate,we will pay full price.
If the price is right,we will sign the contract.
Structure
Component Example
If + subject + present simpleIf you agree to a two-year contract,
subject + will + base verbwe will offer a fifteen percent discount.
Negative: If + subject + do/does notIf we do not negotiate, we will pay full price.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice
"We need to the price before signing."
"The vendor submitted a for the new contract."
"We will make a if the price is too high."
"If you order in bulk, we will offer a ten percent ."
"The payment are net 30 days."
"There is a penalty for late delivery."
"Our volume gives us to negotiate better prices."
"We made a on the delivery timeline."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order
? "If you offer a ten percent discount, we will sign today."
? "Let me prepare the final contract with the agreed terms."
? "Thank you for your proposal. Let me review the terms."
? "On the condition that delivery is guaranteed, we have a deal."
? "The price seems high. We would like to negotiate."
? "We are willing to extend the contract to two years."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking
If the price is too high, we will make a counteroffer.
We are willing to extend the payment terms to 60 days.
If you agree to a two-year contract, we will offer a discount.
There is a penalty clause for late delivery in the contract.
On the condition that delivery is guaranteed, we will sign.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. The price is too high. What do you do?
A "We will accept the price immediately."
B "We will make a counteroffer."
C "We will cancel the meeting."
2. You want the vendor to lower the price. What do you say?
A "We do not want to negotiate."
B "If you offer a discount, we will increase the order."
C "We will pay full price."
3. You want to add a condition. How do you express it?
A "On the condition that delivery is guaranteed."
B "We do not have conditions."
C "The clause is not important."
4. The vendor asks for a longer contract. What do you offer?
A "We will not sign any contract."
B "We are willing to sign for two years."
C "We need more leverage."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are negotiating a logistics contract with a Texas vendor. The price is too high. Use first conditionals to propose terms, make a counteroffer, and reach an agreement.

Survival Card — Lesson 7

1 If the price is right, we will sign the contract.
2 We are willing to negotiate the terms.
3 On the condition that delivery is on time, we agree.
4 We would like to make a counteroffer.
5 If both sides make concessions, we will reach a deal.
Aula 08 — Pre-class

Email Power

Professional Correspondence. Learn formal register to write professional emails: greetings, purpose, attachments, and polite closings.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary

Vocabulary
Regarding • about or concerning a particular subject
"I am writing regarding the Q3 financial report."
Acknowledge • to confirm receipt or recognize something
"I would like to acknowledge receipt of your proposal."
Inquire • to ask for information formally
"I am writing to inquire about the delivery schedule."
Attachment • a file sent along with an email
"Please find the contract attached for your review."
Follow through • to complete an action or commitment
"We will follow through on the action items from the meeting."
Outline • to describe the main points of something
"I would like to outline the key changes to the contract."
Notify • to formally inform someone about something
"I wanted to notify you that the shipment has been delayed."
Forward • to send a received message to another person
"I have forwarded your email to the procurement team."
Expressions
I am writing to...
"I am writing to follow up on our meeting last Thursday."
Please find attached...
"Please find attached the revised contract for your approval."
I look forward to...
"I look forward to hearing from you at your earliest convenience."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice
Regarding
Acknowledge
Inquire
Attachment
Follow through
Outline
Notify
Forward

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar
Maria Claudia is writing an email to her Houston counterpart regarding the new logistics contract. "Dear David, I am writing to follow up on our meeting last Thursday. Please find attached the revised contract with the agreed terms. I would like to outline three key changes: the discount has been increased to ten percent, the delivery clause has been updated, and the payment terms are now net 30. I wanted to notify you that the legal team has reviewed and approved the document. Could you please acknowledge receipt and forward it to your procurement team? I look forward to hearing from you at your earliest convenience. Best regards, Maria Claudia."
1. What is the purpose of Maria Claudia's email?
A To cancel the logistics contract.
B To follow up on a meeting and share a revised contract.
C To schedule a new meeting with David.
D To request a discount from the vendor.
2. How many key changes does Maria Claudia outline?
A Two
B Three
C Four
D Five
3. What does Maria Claudia ask David to do?
A Review the contract with the legal team.
B Acknowledge receipt and forward it to procurement.
C Sign the contract immediately.
D Schedule a call to discuss the terms.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
Formal Register in Professional Emails
Informal Formal
HeyDear Mr./Ms.
ThanksThank you for your consideration
Just wanted to checkI am writing to inquire
Here's the filePlease find attached
Let me knowI look forward to hearing from you
Sorry about thatI apologize for the inconvenience
Professional Email Structure
Section Example
GreetingDear Mr. Chen,
PurposeI am writing regarding the revised contract.
AttachmentPlease find attached the updated document.
ClosingI look forward to hearing from you. Best regards,

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice
"I am writing the Q3 financial report."
"I would like to receipt of your proposal."
"I am writing to about the delivery schedule."
"Please find the contract for your review."
"We will on the action items from the meeting."
"I would like to the key changes to the contract."
"I wanted to you that the shipment has been delayed."
"I have your email to the procurement team."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order
? "I would like to outline three key changes to the terms."
? "Dear Mr. Chen,"
? "I look forward to hearing from you. Best regards, Maria Claudia."
? "Please find attached the updated document for your review."
? "Could you please acknowledge receipt and forward it to your team?"
? "I am writing regarding the revised logistics contract."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking
I am writing to follow up on our meeting last Thursday.
Please find attached the revised contract for your approval.
I would like to outline the key changes to the terms.
Could you please acknowledge receipt of this document?
I look forward to hearing from you at your earliest convenience.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You want to start a formal email about a report. What do you write?
A "Hey, just checking about the report."
B "I am writing regarding the Q3 report."
C "Here's the report you asked for."
2. You are sending a file with your email. What do you write?
A "The file is here."
B "Please find attached the document."
C "I put the file in the email."
3. You want to confirm you received something. What do you write?
A "I would like to acknowledge receipt."
B "Got it, thanks."
C "I saw your email."
4. You want to end an email politely. What do you write?
A "Let me know."
B "I look forward to hearing from you."
C "Talk later."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Write a professional email to David Chen following up on a meeting about the logistics contract. Include: a formal greeting, the purpose of the email, an attachment reference, and a polite closing.

Survival Card — Lesson 8

1 I am writing regarding the revised contract.
2 Please find attached the updated document.
3 I would like to acknowledge receipt of your email.
4 Could you please forward this to the team?
5 I look forward to hearing from you.
Aula 09 — Pre-class

Gallery Night

Socializing About Art and Business. Learn opinion structures to share views, agree, and disagree politely at gallery events and networking situations.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary

Vocabulary
Mingle • to move around and talk to different people at a social event
"At the gallery opening, Maria Claudia likes to mingle with artists and collectors."
Curator • a person who organizes and manages an art exhibition
"The curator selected twenty pieces for the contemporary exhibit."
Collector • a person who acquires and keeps works of art
"The collector purchased three paintings from the Brazilian artist."
Vernissage • an opening event for an art exhibition
"The vernissage attracted over two hundred guests from the business community."
Stunning • extremely beautiful or impressive
"The sculpture in the center of the gallery is absolutely stunning."
Perspective • a particular way of seeing or understanding something
"Art gives us a fresh perspective on everyday life."
Networking • building professional relationships at social events
"Gallery openings are excellent opportunities for networking."
Impression • an opinion or feeling formed about something
"My first impression of the exhibit was very positive."
Expressions
I think/believe/feel that...
"I believe that contemporary art reflects the energy of modern business."
What is your take on...?
"What is your take on this abstract piece?"
If you ask me...
"If you ask me, this is the highlight of the entire collection."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice
Mingle
Curator
Collector
Vernissage
Stunning
Perspective
Networking
Impression

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar
Maria Claudia is attending a gallery vernissage in Houston with colleagues and business partners. She mingles with the curator, who explains the theme of the exhibit. "I think this collection represents the best of contemporary Brazilian art," the curator says. Maria Claudia looks at a stunning abstract sculpture. "I believe it captures the energy of Sao Paulo," she replies. A collector approaches and asks, "What is your take on the large painting in the corner?" Maria Claudia considers her perspective. "If you ask me, I feel it is the most powerful piece here. The composition is bold and the palette is vibrant." The collector agrees: "That is a great point. My first impression was the same."
1. Where is Maria Claudia attending the vernissage?
A In Sao Paulo.
B In Houston.
C In New York.
D In London.
2. What does the curator think about the collection?
A It is overpriced.
B It represents the best of contemporary Brazilian art.
C It needs more sculptures.
D It is too modern for the gallery.
3. What does Maria Claudia feel about the sculpture?
A She thinks it is too abstract.
B She believes it is the most powerful piece there.
C She does not like the colors.
D She wants to buy it.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
Opinion Structures
Structure Example
I think + that + clauseI think that this painting is remarkable.
I believe + that + clauseI believe that contemporary art reflects modern life.
I feel + that + clauseI feel that the composition is very bold.
I consider + noun/clauseI consider this the highlight of the exhibit.
In my view...In my view, the gallery has an impressive collection.
From my perspective...From my perspective, art and business are connected.
As far as I am concerned...As far as I am concerned, networking at galleries is valuable.
Agreeing and Disagreeing
Agreeing Disagreeing politely
I completely agree.I see your point, but...
That is a great point.I understand, however...

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice
"Maria Claudia likes to with artists at gallery openings."
"The selected twenty pieces for the exhibit."
"The purchased three paintings from the Brazilian artist."
"The attracted over two hundred guests."
"The sculpture is absolutely ."
"Art gives us a fresh on everyday life."
"Gallery openings are great for ."
"My first of the exhibit was very positive."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order
? "I believe it is the most striking piece in the collection."
? "Welcome to the vernissage. It is wonderful to see you here."
? "It was a pleasure meeting you. Let us stay in touch."
? "What is your take on the large abstract painting?"
? "If you ask me, the artist has a unique perspective."
? "Have you had a chance to see the main exhibit?"

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking
I believe that contemporary art reflects the energy of modern business.
What is your take on this abstract piece?
If you ask me, this is the highlight of the collection.
My first impression of the exhibit was very positive.
Gallery openings are excellent opportunities for networking.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. Someone asks your opinion at a gallery. What do you say?
A "I do not know anything about art."
B "I believe this is a stunning piece."
C "Art is not important."
2. You want to ask someone's opinion about a painting. What do you say?
A "Do you like colors?"
B "What is your take on this painting?"
C "Tell me the price."
3. You want to express a strong opinion about a piece. What do you say?
A "If you ask me, it is the best in the collection."
B "I have no opinion."
C "It is just a painting."
4. You agree with someone's view about the exhibit. What do you say?
A "I do not care."
B "That is a great point. I completely agree."
C "You are wrong."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are at a gallery vernissage in Houston. You meet a collector. Describe a painting you admire, share your opinion, and ask for theirs.

Survival Card — Lesson 9

1 I believe this is a stunning piece of art.
2 What is your take on the exhibit?
3 If you ask me, the composition is remarkable.
4 It was a pleasure meeting you tonight.
5 Gallery openings are great for networking.
Aula 10 — Pre-class

Review and Integration

Block 1 — Full Scenario. Review and integrate vocabulary and grammar from all previous 9 lessons in a real business dinner and art gallery context.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Review

Vocabulary
Oversee • to supervise or manage
"Maria Claudia oversees the financial operations of the holding."
Subsidiary • a company owned by a larger company
"The holding has three subsidiaries in different sectors."
Facilitate • to guide or manage a discussion
"She facilitates the quarterly review meetings."
Forecast • a prediction of future results
"The forecast for Q4 shows steady growth."
Composition • the arrangement of elements in art
"The composition of this painting is remarkable."
Evaluate • to assess quality or value
"Performance is evaluated twice a year."
Leverage • an advantage in negotiation
"Our volume gives us leverage to negotiate."
Integrate • to combine different elements into a whole
"Maria Claudia integrates art and business in everything she does."
Expressions
Looking back at...
"Looking back at this quarter, our results exceeded expectations."
Overall, I would say...
"Overall, I would say the holding is in a strong position."
To summarize...
"To summarize, we achieved growth in all three subsidiaries."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice
Oversee
Subsidiary
Facilitate
Forecast
Composition
Evaluate
Leverage
Integrate

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar
Maria Claudia is hosting a business dinner for her Houston and Pittsburgh counterparts at an art gallery in Sao Paulo. She oversees the evening's agenda. "Let's begin with a toast," she says. "Looking back at this year, our revenue is higher than last year by fifteen percent. The forecast for next quarter is positive. Reports are submitted on time, and every action item has been completed. I believe we have the leverage to negotiate even better contracts next year. If we continue at this pace, we will exceed our annual targets." She then turns to the art on the walls. "I find this composition absolutely stunning. In my opinion, it is the highlight of the gallery. What is your take on it, David?" Overall, the evening integrates business discussion with art appreciation.
1. What event is Maria Claudia hosting?
A A board meeting.
B A business dinner at an art gallery.
C A training session.
D A product launch.
2. By how much did revenue increase compared to last year?
A Ten percent.
B Fifteen percent.
C Twenty percent.
D Five percent.
3. What does Maria Claudia say about the composition on the wall?
A She thinks it is overpriced.
B She finds it absolutely stunning.
C She wants to buy it.
D She does not like it.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
Comprehensive Review — All 9 Structures
Lesson Structure Use
1Present simple / continuousDaily routines, current projects
2Possessives + there is/areDescribing company structure
3Imperatives + let'sRunning meetings
4Comparatives + numbersFinancial reports
5Adjective order + I find itDescribing art
6Present passiveProcesses and procedures
7First conditionalNegotiation
8Formal registerProfessional emails
9Opinion structuresExpressing views
Key Patterns
Pattern Example
Simple present for factsShe oversees the financial operations.
Comparative for dataRevenue is higher than last year.
Passive for processesReports are submitted on time.
First conditional for plansIf we continue, we will exceed our targets.
Opinion + I find itI find this composition absolutely stunning.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice
"Maria Claudia the financial operations of the holding."
"Revenue is than last year by fifteen percent."
"Let's with a review of the quarterly results."
"Reports are to the board every quarter."
"If we continue at this pace, we exceed our targets."
"I this painting is absolutely stunning."
"I am writing the revised contract."
"Maria Claudia art and business in everything she does."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order
? "Looking back, our revenue is higher than last year."
? "To summarize, we achieved our goals in business and enjoyed great art tonight."
? "Good evening, everyone. Welcome to this special dinner."
? "Now, let me show you the art collection. I find this piece stunning."
? "Let's begin with a toast to a successful quarter."
? "The forecast shows continued growth for all subsidiaries."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking
Looking back at this quarter, our results exceeded expectations.
If we continue at this pace, we will exceed our annual targets.
I find this composition absolutely stunning.
Reports are submitted on time and every action item is completed.
To summarize, we achieved growth in all three subsidiaries.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. How do you describe company performance using a comparative?
A "Revenue is good last year."
B "Revenue is higher than last year."
C "Revenue is the highest."
2. How do you open a toast at a business dinner?
A "We need to discuss money now."
B "Let's begin with a toast to a successful quarter."
C "I want to eat first."
3. How do you express an opinion about art?
A "I find this piece absolutely stunning."
B "Art is not important."
C "I do not understand art."
4. How do you close a summary at the end of a dinner?
A "That is all, goodbye."
B "To summarize, we achieved our goals."
C "I have nothing to say."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are hosting a business dinner for your Houston and Pittsburgh counterparts at a gallery in Sao Paulo. Give a short speech: toast the quarter's results, describe a painting on the wall, and close with a summary of the evening.

Survival Card — Lesson 10

1 Looking back, our results exceeded expectations.
2 If we continue at this pace, we will succeed.
3 I find this absolutely stunning.
4 To summarize, we achieved all our goals.
5 It was a pleasure hosting you tonight.
Aula 11 — Pre-class

Conference Calls

Block 2 — Managing Tech, Time Zones. Polite requests with could you, would you mind for conference calls across Houston, Pittsburgh, and Sao Paulo.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary

Vocabulary
Dial in • to join a conference call by phone or video
"Could everyone please dial in five minutes early?"
Mute • to turn off your microphone during a call
"Would you mind muting your microphone when you are not speaking?"
Screen share • to show your computer screen to others during a call
"Could you screen share the presentation, please?"
Bandwidth • the capacity of an internet connection; also used figuratively for capacity
"We do not have the bandwidth to take on another project right now."
Time zone • the geographic region where a specific standard time is used
"Houston is one hour behind Sao Paulo's time zone."
Lag • a delay in audio or video during a call
"There is a slight lag on the line. Could you repeat that?"
Drop off • to lose connection during a call unexpectedly
"I apologize, I dropped off the call for a moment."
Follow-up • a subsequent action or communication after a meeting
"I will send a follow-up email with the action items."
Expressions
Could you please...?
"Could you please share your screen so we can see the numbers?"
Would you mind...?
"Would you mind repeating that? There was a lag on the line."
I apologize for...
"I apologize for the technical difficulties. Let me reconnect."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice
Dial in
Mute
Screen share
Bandwidth
Time zone
Lag
Drop off
Follow-up

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar
Maria Claudia is leading a conference call with her counterparts in Houston and Pittsburgh. The call starts at 10 AM Sao Paulo time, which is 8 AM in Houston. "Could you please confirm you can hear me clearly?" she begins. David has a lag on his connection. "Would you mind repeating the agenda, Maria Claudia? There was a slight delay," he asks. She screen shares the Q3 presentation. "Could you all see my screen?" A participant drops off and reconnects. "I apologize for dropping off. My bandwidth was low," they explain. Maria Claudia handles it smoothly: "No problem. Would you mind muting your microphone when you are not speaking? It helps with the audio quality." She closes: "I will send a follow-up email with all the action items."
1. What time does the call start in Houston?
A 10 AM.
B 8 AM.
C 9 AM.
D 7 AM.
2. Why does David ask Maria Claudia to repeat the agenda?
A He was not paying attention.
B There was a lag on his connection.
C He joined the call late.
D His microphone was muted.
3. Why did a participant drop off the call?
A They had another meeting.
B Their bandwidth was low.
C They did not want to continue.
D Maria Claudia ended the call.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
Polite Requests — Could you / Would you mind
Structure Formula Example
Could you + base verb?Could you + verb (infinitive)?Could you send the report?
Would you mind + verb-ing?Would you mind + verb-ing?Would you mind sending the report?
Would it be possible to + base verb?Would it be possible to + verb?Would it be possible to reschedule the call?
Key Differences
Direct Request Polite Request
Send the report.Could you send the report?
Mute your microphone.Would you mind muting your microphone?
Repeat that.Would it be possible to repeat that?

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice
"Could everyone please five minutes early?"
"Would you mind your microphone?"
"Could you the presentation, please?"
"We do not have the for another project."
"Houston is one hour behind our ."
"There is a slight on the line."
"I apologize, I the call for a moment."
"I will send a email with the action items."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order
? "Could you please mute your microphone when not speaking?"
? "I apologize for the technical issue. I am back now."
? "Good morning, everyone. Can you hear me clearly?"
? "I will send a follow-up email with all action items. Thank you."
? "Let me share my screen so you can see the agenda."
? "Would you mind repeating that? There was a lag."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking
Could you please share your screen so we can see the numbers?
Would you mind repeating that? There was a lag on the line.
I apologize for the technical difficulties.
Houston is one hour behind Sao Paulo's time zone.
I will send a follow-up email with the action items.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You cannot hear someone clearly on the call. What do you say?
A "Talk louder now."
B "Could you please speak louder? There is a lag."
C "I cannot hear anything."
2. Someone's microphone has background noise. What do you say?
A "Turn that off!"
B "Would you mind muting your microphone?"
C "Your microphone is too loud."
3. You lost connection briefly and came back. What do you say?
A "I apologize, I dropped off the call for a moment."
B "My internet is bad."
C "Sorry, I left."
4. You want someone to show a document on screen. What do you say?
A "Show me the document."
B "Could you please screen share the report?"
C "Where is the document?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are leading a conference call with Houston and Pittsburgh. There are technical issues. How do you handle them politely? Use "could you" and "would you mind" to manage the call.

Survival Card — Lesson 11

1 Could you please repeat that?
2 Would you mind muting your microphone?
3 I apologize for the technical issue.
4 Could you screen share the presentation?
5 I will send a follow-up email after the call.
Aula 12 — Pre-class

Fiscal and Tax Talk

Block 2 — Communicating With Accountants. Present perfect with for/since for discussing fiscal year results, tax returns, audits, and compliance.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary

Vocabulary
Tax return • an official document filed with the government reporting income and taxes
"We have filed our tax return for the fiscal year."
Audit • a formal examination of financial records
"The company has been under audit since January."
Deduction • an expense subtracted from taxable income
"We have claimed several deductions for business travel."
Compliance • following rules, laws, or regulations
"The holding has maintained compliance with tax regulations for five years."
Liability • a legal or financial obligation
"Our tax liability has decreased since we restructured."
Fiscal year • a twelve-month period used for accounting purposes
"Our fiscal year has ended on December 31st since 2018."
Revenue stream • a source of income for a company
"We have diversified our revenue streams since the acquisition."
Write-off • an expense that reduces taxable income
"The accountant has recommended a write-off for the old equipment."
Expressions
We have been... for/since...
"We have been working with this accounting firm for three years."
Since the restructuring...
"Since the restructuring, our tax liability has decreased significantly."
For the past...
"For the past two quarters, revenue has exceeded projections."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice
Tax return
Audit
Deduction
Compliance
Liability
Fiscal year
Revenue stream
Write-off

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar
Maria Claudia is meeting with the company's accountant to review the fiscal year. "We have filed our tax return for this fiscal year," the accountant explains. "The company has maintained compliance with all regulations since the restructuring in 2022. We have claimed several deductions for business travel and equipment. Our tax liability has decreased significantly since last year. The audit team has been reviewing our records for the past three months. They have not found any issues. We have diversified our revenue streams since the acquisition of the Texas subsidiary. The accountant has recommended a write-off for the old logistics equipment."
1. How long has the audit team been reviewing the records?
A Since January.
B For the past three months.
C For one year.
D Since last quarter.
2. When did the company start maintaining compliance?
A For five years.
B Since the restructuring in 2022.
C Since last month.
D For three months.
3. What did the accountant recommend?
A A new audit.
B Increasing the tax liability.
C A write-off for the old logistics equipment.
D Filing the tax return again.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
Present Perfect + for / since
Structure Formula Example
Subject + have/has + past participle + for (duration)We have worked + for five yearsWe have worked with this firm for five years.
Subject + have/has + past participle + since (point in time)We have worked + since 2021We have worked with this firm since 2021.
for vs. since
for (duration) since (point in time)
for three yearssince January
for six monthssince 2020
for the past two quarterssince the restructuring

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice
"We have filed our tax for the fiscal year."
"The company has been under since January."
"We have claimed several for business travel."
"The holding has maintained for five years."
"Our tax has decreased since we restructured."
"Our year ends on December 31st."
"We have diversified our revenue since the acquisition."
"The accountant recommended a for the old equipment."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order
? "The audit team has been reviewing our records for three months."
? "We have filed the tax return with the government."
? "We have maintained full compliance with all tax regulations."
? "Let me review the financial records for this fiscal year."
? "Our tax liability has decreased since the restructuring."
? "We have claimed all eligible deductions."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking
We have filed our tax return for this fiscal year.
The company has maintained compliance since the restructuring.
Our tax liability has decreased since last year.
We have diversified our revenue streams since the acquisition.
The audit team has been reviewing our records for three months.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. How long have you worked with your accountant?
A "We work with them since a long time."
B "We have worked with this firm for five years."
C "We are working with them for five years."
2. When did the audit start?
A "The audit has been going on since January."
B "The audit is going on since January."
C "The audit goes on for January."
3. What happened after the restructuring?
A "Our tax liability decreased since the restructuring."
B "Our tax liability has decreased since the restructuring."
C "Our tax liability is decreasing for the restructuring."
4. What did the accountant recommend?
A "A new fiscal year for the old equipment."
B "A write-off for the old equipment."
C "An audit for the old equipment."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are meeting with your accountant to review the fiscal year. Describe the company's tax situation using present perfect with for and since.

Survival Card — Lesson 12

1 We have filed our tax return for this fiscal year.
2 The company has maintained compliance since 2022.
3 Our tax liability has decreased since the restructuring.
4 We have been working with this firm for five years.
5 The audit has been in progress for three months.
Aula 13 — Pre-class

Making Proposals — Pitching Ideas

Block 3 — Pitching Ideas. I would like to suggest/propose/recommend + noun/gerund/that clause for presenting business proposals, timelines, and ROI to stakeholders.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary

Vocabulary
Pitch • to present an idea persuasively
"Maria Claudia will pitch the expansion plan to the board."
Propose • to formally suggest a plan
"I would like to propose a new partnership with Houston."
Feasible • possible and practical
"The board wants to know if the project is feasible."
Stakeholder • a person with interest in a project
"All stakeholders must approve the proposal."
Timeline • a schedule of events
"The project timeline spans six months."
ROI • return on investment
"The projected ROI is twenty percent."
Scalable • able to grow efficiently
"We need a scalable solution."
Implementation • putting a plan into action
"The implementation phase begins in Q2."
Expressions
I would like to propose...
"I would like to propose a phased implementation for the Miami hub."
What I am suggesting is...
"What I am suggesting is a six-month timeline with clear milestones."
The key benefit is...
"The key benefit is a twenty percent ROI within the first year."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice
Pitch
Propose
Feasible
Stakeholder
Timeline
ROI
Scalable
Implementation

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar
Maria Claudia is pitching a proposal to the board of directors for a new logistics hub in Miami. She would like to propose a phased implementation plan. The timeline spans six months, starting in Q2. She has consulted all key stakeholders, including the Houston and Pittsburgh subsidiaries. The projected ROI is twenty percent within the first year. The board wants to know if the plan is feasible and scalable across other subsidiaries. Maria Claudia recommends starting with a feasibility study before committing to full implementation.
1. What is the projected ROI for the Miami hub?
A Ten percent.
B Twenty percent within the first year.
C Thirty percent over two years.
D Fifteen percent after six months.
2. What does the board want to know about the plan?
A If it is profitable immediately.
B If it is feasible and scalable across other subsidiaries.
C If the Houston team agrees.
D If it can be completed in three months.
3. What does Maria Claudia recommend before full implementation?
A Hiring a new team.
B Closing the Houston subsidiary.
C Starting with a feasibility study.
D Increasing the budget by fifty percent.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
I would like to suggest / propose / recommend
Structure Formula Example
+ nounI would like to propose + a partnershipI would like to propose a new partnership.
+ gerund (-ing)I would like to suggest + startingI would like to suggest starting with a pilot project.
+ that clauseI would like to recommend + that we reviewI would like to recommend that we review the timeline.
Key differences
Verb Nuance
suggestsofter, more tentative
proposeformal, structured plan
recommendbased on expertise or evidence

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice
"Maria Claudia will the new logistics hub to the board."
"I would like to a partnership with a Miami-based firm."
"The board needs to determine if the expansion is ."
"All have been consulted about the new plan."
"The project includes three major milestones."
"The expected justifies the initial investment."
"We need a model that works across all subsidiaries."
"The phase will begin after board approval."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order
? "The projected ROI is twenty percent within the first year."
? "We recommend starting with a feasibility study."
? "I would like to propose a new logistics hub in Miami."
? "The solution is scalable across all subsidiaries."
? "What I am suggesting is a phased approach over six months."
? "The key benefit is a strong return on investment for all stakeholders."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking
I would like to propose a new logistics hub in Miami.
The projected ROI is twenty percent within the first year.
We need a scalable solution that works across subsidiaries.
The implementation phase begins in Q2 with a six-month timeline.
All stakeholders have been consulted and support the proposal.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You want to formally present a new idea to the board. What do you say?
A "I want that we do this project."
B "I would like to propose a phased implementation plan."
C "We must doing this project now."
2. The board asks about the expected return. What is the best response?
A "The projected ROI is twenty percent within the first year."
B "The ROI is going to be very good, I think."
C "I am not sure about the numbers yet."
3. You want to suggest a careful approach. What do you say?
A "We should just start and see what happens."
B "I would like to recommend that we begin with a feasibility study."
C "I recommend to start immediately."
4. A colleague asks if the plan can grow. What is the correct response?
A "Yes, it is very big already."
B "Yes, the solution is scalable across all subsidiaries."
C "Yes, we can scale it down if needed."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are pitching a new project to the board. Describe your proposal using "I would like to propose/suggest/recommend." Include the timeline, ROI, stakeholders, and why it is feasible and scalable.

Survival Card — Lesson 13

1 I would like to propose a phased approach for the Miami hub.
2 The key benefit is a twenty percent return on investment.
3 The project timeline spans six months starting in Q2.
4 We need to confirm that the plan is feasible and scalable.
5 The implementation will begin after stakeholder approval.
Aula 17 -- Pre-class

Data-Driven -- Describing Trends and Numbers

Describe charts and figures with trend verbs (rose, fell, peaked, plateaued, dipped), adverbs of speed (sharply, gradually, slightly), and prepositions of change (rose to a final value, fell by a difference, grew from X to Y). New vocabulary: surge, plummet, peak, plateau, dip, fluctuate, steady, sharp, gradual, trend line.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Surge -- a fast, strong increase in a number or value
"Sales surged in the first quarter of the year." (Disparada / forte alta)
Plummet -- to drop quickly and sharply
"Demand plummeted after the price increase." (Despencar / cair vertiginosamente)
Peak -- the highest value a figure reaches; to reach that point
"Sales reached a peak of fifty-eight million in Q3." (Pico / atingir o pico)
Plateau -- to level off and stay at the same value for a time
"Growth plateaued in the middle of the year." (Estabilizar / estagnar)
Dip -- a small fall before the value recovers
"The line dipped a little in the fourth quarter." (Pequena queda / recuo)
Fluctuate -- to change irregularly, rising and falling repeatedly
"The exchange rate fluctuated all year." (Oscilar / flutuar)
Steady -- constant and regular; changing at an even pace
"Costs showed a steady rise of one percent a month." (Constante / estável)
Sharp -- sudden and large; a fast, steep change
"There was a sharp rise in revenue early in the year." (Acentuado / abrupto)
Gradual -- slow, smooth, and steady; the opposite of sharp
"Costs climbed gradually, with no sudden jumps." (Gradual / aos poucos)
Trend line -- a line on a chart showing the overall direction over time
"The trend line points up, despite the small dips." (Linha de tendência)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Surge
Plummet
Peak
Plateau
Dip
Fluctuate
Steady
Sharp
Gradual
Trend line

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The quarterly chart tells a clear story. Sales rose sharply in the first quarter, from forty to fifty-two million reais -- a real surge. Then growth plateaued: in Q2 the figure increased only slightly. In Q3 sales reached a peak, climbing to fifty-eight million, the highest point of the year. After that the line dipped by three million in Q4 -- a small fall, not a plummet. Costs, by contrast, climbed gradually, a steady rise of about one percent a month, while the exchange rate fluctuated with no clear direction. Notice the pattern: the verb gives the direction, the adverb gives the speed, and the preposition gives the figure -- to for the final value, by for the difference. Despite the dips, the trend line still points up.

1. Why does the text say "rose to fifty-eight million"?
A Because fifty-eight million is the final value the line reached.
B Because fifty-eight million is the size of the increase.
C Because fifty-eight million is the lowest point.
2. Why does the text say "dipped by three million"?
A Because three million is the final value.
B Because three million is the difference -- how much it fell.
C Because the line crashed completely.
3. How did costs behave compared with sales?
A They climbed gradually, a steady rise with no sudden jumps.
B They surged and then plummeted.
C They fluctuated with no direction.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Verb + Adverb + Preposition

GRAMMAR

Para descrever um gráfico, combine três coisas: o VERBO de tendência dá a direção (rose, fell, peaked), o ADVÉRBIO dá a velocidade (sharply, gradually, slightly) e a PREPOSIÇÃO liga ao número (to = valor final, by = a diferença, from X to Y = os dois extremos). / To describe a chart, combine a trend verb (direction), an adverb (speed), and a preposition (the figure): to for the final value, by for the difference, from X to Y for both ends.

PartJobExample
Verb (direction)rose / fell / peaked / dipped"sales rose"
Adverb (speed)sharply / gradually / slightly"rose sharply"
to + final valuewhere the line ended"rose to 52 million"
by + differencehow much it changed"fell by 3 million"
from X to Y / at + peakboth ends / the high point"grew from 40 to 52m"; "peaked at 58m"

Key point / Ponto-chave: use to para o valor final ("rose to 52m" = onde terminou) e by para a diferença ("rose by 12m" = quanto mudou). O advérbio modifica o verbo: "rose sharp" → "rose sharply". / Use to for the final value and by for the difference. The adverb modifies the verb: not "rose sharp" but "rose sharply".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct preposition or word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Sales rose fifty-two million reais."
"Profit fell three million in the fourth quarter."
